author: @nerminhamidovic638
text: More to the point i made on an earlier video. The o-ring even in an loaded system may cause back emf problems between coils. Also those are probably cheap diodes and may have large forward voltage 1.1V.  Nowhere near as good as power silicon schottky or zener rectifiers which may have Vf of 0.5 to 0.7V. These losses become important in micro harvesting situtations but also at higher loads.  Also even if your wind reaches the 10m/s there is not enough input to generate 1kw which was your original now renamed project title.   However don't let that throw you off. The GE MW off shore wind turbines are specified for 25 to 55mph wind speed with (gearing etc to flatten out maximum power delivery). So say you're able to also do this. i.e your VAWT is able to operate in Gale force winds at 55mph you'll have about 4kw  available energy. Is your generator going to deliver... Keep going please.. but do rewire that stator.

author: @2Langdon
text: Great stuff! A very useful series, many thanks. I've built something slightly similar, using two lightweight bicycle wheels top and bottom, connected by 7 vertical pieces of 114mm plastic gutter to form the rotor, 74cm diam by 75 cm high. I used good quality bike bearings, but want to try magnetic bearings for really low friction. Haven't done the electrics yet, but the aim is to charge a 12v lead acid system to run lighting in a shed.

author: @jjackson3240
text: Maybe this has already been pointed out, but I believe you are confusing power and energy. Your capacitors store energy in watt hours. Your windmill creates power in watts. So are you saying that the average amount of power(in watts) produced for that period of time(watts times time equal energy) is the same as the amount that was stored in the capacitors? Or was the max power available at any one time while the machine was spinning the same number as the total energy stored? I've watched a few of your videos so I know that you know the difference and I apologize if I am misinterpreting something.

author: @mrgieltoonders
text:
Can someone help me understand that betz law?
Betz law says that at a given wind power there is a maximum efficiency 59.3% and thus a maximum available wind power (at least at that omnicalculator website).
But let's say the turbine in this video is already at 55% turbine efficiency, it will double the power when added a ring of coils at the top. It is then beating Betz law, which should not be possible to my understanding.

I strongly feel that I am forgetting a part or thinking of it in the wrong way.

replies.author: @1967spark
replies.text: Betz law applies to how much of the energy from the wind can be extracted. Adding more coils will not increase the power removed (unless the existing coils were not capable of extracting the available energy) Think of each coil acting as a brake on the turbine, you can spread the braking force around the entire turbine, or just in one spot, but ultimately the same force is applied when you are converting the mechanical energy to electrical energy.

replies.author: @1967spark
replies.text: To understand Betz law think about what the air flow would be behind the turbine if 100% of the energy was extracted.

replies.author: @mrgieltoonders
replies.text: @1967spark Thanks for the explanation. I didn't realize the coils (with no core other than air) worked as a break.

author: @fjpavm
text: I think the available energy formula will have non-linear terms of wind speed. Averaging wind speed in that case does not properly approximate the sum of the energy at the different speeds for shorter time periods. Let's take the example of a quadratic term with half the time wind speed 1 and half the time wind speed 2, the average speed would be 1.5 but the contribution in the formula would be (1^2)/2 + (2^2)/2 = 1/2 + 4/2 = 2.5. If the terms are higher than quadratic the distortion grows even bigger.

author: @custos3249

author: @Rcbeacon
text: Sorry to be a downer but the wind calculator gave you an instantaneous power of 0.013W (13mW) that over 1 hour would give you 13mW/hr.  Charging capacitors with no additional load is accumulating less power over a period of time.  To maintain the static voltage you only have to replace the capacitor leakage.  This is not an indication of efficiency.  I would like to see a load connected, such as a 10W lamp and the unit spun up manually.  You could motorize the spin-up and get a better idea of the generation efficiency.

I don't want to hurt someone's feelings. 
It appears to me however that you don't seem to mind hurting mine, to be honest telling others what words to use is a little patronising.

One big difference, the meaning of "Mens rea" is clearly defined and you can be either correct or incorrect in it's usage but deciding if something is rude or not is of course a matter of opinion, both of the person making the comment and that of the person who reads it. That opinion is likely to be based on culture, context and personality and is not really quantifiable.

Please take this notice as it was intended - that is to remind you to maintain the community with respect for each other and care in making postings - if you are unhappy with this leave a thumbs down and stop posting - if you continue to be rude you will be banned - I would think this would be a shame as your posts are valuable and well thought out but on occasion thoughtless for the sensibilities of others - a right to say what you think comes with a responsibility to consider other peoples feelings when you are speaking. It is true I am not considering your feelings right now but the feelings of others in the community and for that you are being admonished - if you don't like that stop behaving in a way that requires admonishment and take this reply in the spirit in which it is meant - Rob

author: @jasonwitt8619
text: A real electrician already knew what you meant mate, lol No need to apologize mate and yep if it had not been microfarads, each one would be the size of a coffee can, lol. I find it funny that someone actually said something to you in comment to the point of you feeling that you need to apologize and correct it. Some people never cease to amaze me. I wish Youtube would start allowing screenshots in comment. I would love to show you a sketch in the way of what my research on creating a generator was taught me in the way of setting it up to get the most possible with "no cogging". The concept is rather simple really, what you do is face all the magnets on the wheel in one direction, either all south or all north, then you trap the field by putting a coil with a iron core on each side of the magnets on the wheel, these coils are not connected to the wheel, then to stop the cogging you put another magnet on the back side of the core that faces the opposite direction as the field of the passing magnet. It makes the whole build cost more but it makes it very powerful and each core creates around 30 volts per coil set. You would do this all the way round the wheel and run the coils in series. At the end you are left with one beast of a generator in power. Keep in mind, this is not an idea but is 100% facts that I have done and it works outstandingly but you would have to use Neos if you want it to work right. For some reason, the standard magnet does not produce the results nowhere near as well at all. Cheers mate

author: @testacc9321
text:
Really nice videos, keep on doing! 
I'd like to offer the way to better estimate power by properly measuring wind speed.
1. You're using a directional anemometer, so direction is crucial here. To get accurate reading it should be coupled with a weather vane to be automatically pointing in the right direction.
2. Place to measure, ideally right before the drum in the middle. 2nd best place on top of the drum.
3. Wind quality is very important. For reliable measurements laminar flow is the key, means open space needed. In turbulent flow, like on the most of the videos, no precise measurement possible at all, just random numbers.

Hope it helps on estimating the real efficiency of the generator. 
Looking forward to see more videos on this really interesting topic!

author: @deslomeslager
text: The device can not be very efficient, because it only harvests energy at the top of the voltage. I mean: the capacitors will only be charged if the speed of the device is high enough. So there have been many rotations, not transferring any energy to the caps, since the voltage was already higher that what was generated. As soon as the energy is being used (drained), the efficiency can go up. This is also the reason why it was freewheeling so often at the end of the video. There just could not be any drag (if any), since the caps were at its fullest already. (I am not saying they were filled 100%, it is just time related, I hope you understand my way of thinking/explaining)

author: @totherarf
text:
Not certain how you did your calculation, but ...... 

Don't forget you are charging those cats at a healthy rate, you get a pulse every time a magnet passes a coil (not sure of your magnet count .... or the number of coils you have in that quarter you populated). Also you have your  voltage measured in DC whereas it is actually a smoothed and rectified AC ..... not much difference, But when you are measuring usable power it is the decay curve of those compounded capacitors, not just how much charge they hold!

As ever an interesting topic!

author: @robinvince616
text: Sorry Robert, but you can't read any meaningful conclusions on power or efficiency from the charge in the capacitors. That is just showing the VOLTAGE output. You MUST (repeat MUST) connect a resistive load, so the thing is actually delivering real POWER (heat!). Unfortunately, an LED load like you've used in the past will not prove anything either, since LEDs will always light (to a degree) once their forward voltage is exceeded. Also a proper load is essential when you want to know how easily it will spin under low wind conditions. Unless it is horribly inefficient, you should expect it to slow down, since it will now have WORK to do.

replies.author: @nicoleyensen7062

replies.author: @charlesdickens6706
replies.text: @nicoleyensen7062 ...I gotta say that the early sixties book " The students book of basic electricity and magnetism " is very good. It actually gives the best explanation of tuning by use of inductance and capacitance I've come across . Then the videos of professor Julius Sumner Miller are good too and the algorithm will then offer other good choices such as the 60s demonstrations by that British physicist .

replies.author: @robinvince616
replies.text: Sorry Nicole, I learned my basic theory in school back in the 1960s, so I have no knowledge of modern books on the subject. I'm a retired TV engineer, and more into ham radio and electronics these days. If you search for "Google Books: Electrical theory", you will find some suggestions. You can then search within them for certain topics and see if you can find one that feels right for you. Good luck!

replies.author: @charlesdickens6706
replies.text:
......when Rob demonstrated the the windmill with the mot primary coils ,then100 ma was measured lighting up the led arrays. He tried a number of coils which he showed some and ticked through the others he'd tried on his own . For led purposes a resistance in the coil windings can't be avoided if seeking the minimum 28 volts turn on voltage. Maybe thicker gauge wire would reduce resistance just a little but it's all about trade offs and priorities of cost ,efficiency, weight, balancing, time........He never claimed to have all the answers and has made it clear that the design is malleable to the vagaries and contingencies of materials, location , wealth, climate......
 I know you can generate at a lower voltage then use efficient switch mode circuitry to boost voltage but it adds complexity and extra reliability problems , the individual designer can navigate their own design priorities . For my part I avoid switch mode circuitry cos of the radio interference .

author: @dexterdixon2000
text: I used the VAWT section of omnicalculator with a diameter of 0.8m, height of 0.8m, windspeed of 0.4m/s and got 0.025W power available. If you charged 14x0.001F capacitors to 8V you made 0.5J. If this is only a quarter of your alternator you could maybe make 2J.  (Edit - no, you can't assume this and we should stick with 0.5J for now) Your test ran for 10 minutes which is 600s so your average power was 0.5/600 = 0.000833333W. This means you had an overall efficiency of 3.3% which isnt actually as bad as it sounds but also isn't 100%. If you assume 20% efficiency for the rotor (that's generous but possible for a crossflow rotor) and 5% wake loss you've got 0.005W available in which case your alternator is 15% efficient which will hopefully go up when you add more coils (but you'll be adding more winding resistance too...). I hope these numbers scale up to it delivering decent power and energy.  This is all far too much extrapolation from a tiny amount of data gained in a not very scientific way and an improper load - get it on a resistive loadand get some proper data!

replies.author: @1967spark
replies.text: Are you sure that you can extrapolate that using more coils will extract more energy?

replies.author: @dexterdixon2000
replies.text: @1967spark no. You're right! It will likely bump up the efficiency a bit but I agree - I can't assume that. I've edited my original comment to reflect this. Thanks!

author: @BensWorkshop
text:
Capacitors do not store power, they store energy. Power is energy over time. If you were to discharge those capacitors at say 10 volts in say 1 millionth of a second the power (for a very short time) would be 700KW. On the other hand if you were to discharge it over an hour it would be it would be micro-Watts.  The formula for energy stored in a capacitor is E=CV^2 (Energy is capacitance x volts squared). If you have capacitors in parallel they add up so each 1000 micro Farad cap is 0.001 Farads (1 milli Farad) you have 14 at the moment so have .014 Farads. 10 Volts gives an energy of 100*0.014 = 0.7 Joules. 

As for efficiency, if the available power was .013 Watts then you should be gathering .013 Joules every second so if you ran it for a minute you'd have 0.78 joules. (And oddly just over 10 Volts.

replies.author: @Arachnoid_of_the_underverse
replies.text: 700KW !!  crikey thats efficient.

replies.author: @DozenDeuce
replies.text: @Benedict White  As someone new to working with electricity I was wondering if you could help me out; you kinda lost me towards the end of your equations. Why would 10V mean you multiply 0.014 by 100 and not 10? Also, why does 100*0.014=0.7 and not 1.4? Finally, how does .013W factor in to the previous equation? Thanks

replies.author: @BensWorkshop
replies.text:
@DozenDeuce Sorry there was a missing part to the equation, a divide by 2. The reason it is by one hundred not 10 is because it is the square of the voltage, which is what ^2 means typed on a computer. If you were writing it it would be V with a little 2 in the top right hand corner. The formula is E=(CV^2)/2.

Hope that helps.

author: @ffaubert1
text: So 1000 micro farads is 1 farad, correct? When you say "fully populated", do you mean coils all the way around both top and bottom? I'm just wondering because what you have now would represent 1/8th of the total available power generation wouldn't it? Sorry, but I'm learning.

replies.author: @TerryGilsenan
replies.text: 1000 microfarads == 1 milifarad.

replies.author: @1967spark
replies.text:
My understanding of the physics involved is that it doesn't represent 1/8th of the available power for generation.
A more even distribution of coils would likely be more efficient, but the current arrangement of coils uses all the available energy from the impeller (ignoring the friction losses of course)
The energy transferred into the capacitors comes from the movement of the magnetic field through the conductors, which acts as a braking force on the impeller.

replies.author: @dexterdixon2000
replies.text: @1967spark interesting. Ive just been blindly assuming more coils with linearly increase output but you're right - there will be diminishing returns and the power limit is set by the rotor area.

replies.author: @ffaubert1
replies.text: @1967spark So how does that work? The electricity is induced by having a magnet pass over a coil, correct? The north-south arrangement of the magnets means that each coil a pair passes over generates a full cycle, is that correct? If so, the addition of more coils around the base (from what I saw, only a 1/4 was done) and the addition of coils and magnets at the top of the rotor would create a linear power improvement wouldn't it? The way I heard the explanation was each coil was independent of its neighbours. Did I get that wrong? Is there an 'Electronics for dummies' some place on the 'net? Google is NOT being my friend. As a matter of fact, the way it's treating me right now, you'd think I slept with its sister.
